news.title: Wallabies v France: How to watch Test in Australia, teams, fixtures and more

news.source: ABC News & Headlines – Australian Broadcasting Corporation

sections.heading: Seruvakula demands tactical improvement
sections.body: Interim coach Senirusi Seruvakula has stressed that his side must show greater discipline and smarter decision-making following their opening defeat to Wales. While the team intends to maintain its signature attacking brand of rugby, the focus for this round remains on refining those critical tactical areas against England. Captain Tevita Ikanivere continues to lead the squad despite recent external criticism regarding team investment.

sections.heading: Coverage remains silent on squad fitness
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the Fiji squad. No specific players have been reported as unavailable for the upcoming fixture at the Hill Dickinson Stadium, and no internal updates concerning training fitness have been released by the camp ahead of today's match.

sections.heading: England names three potential debutants
sections.body: Steve Borthwick has made two changes to his starting side, bringing in Henry Slade and Guy Pepper. England has also named three uncapped players on the bench: George Kloska, Benhard Janse van Rensburg, and Noah Caluori. Captain Jamie George has highlighted the athletic ability of Caluori as the side looks to recover from their recent form in their second tournament fixture.

sections.heading: Improving standing after opening loss
sections.body: Fiji enters this second round of the tournament sitting 10th on the log following a 24-39 loss to Wales. With no points currently secured, a positive result today is essential to improve the team's position. Following this match, the squad will continue their Nations Championship campaign as they look to build momentum during their time in the UK.

sections.heading: Borthwick makes two changes to starting XV
sections.body: Steve Borthwick has confirmed two personnel changes to the starting side that played South Africa. Henry Slade and Guy Pepper enter the lineup for the match at Hill Dickinson Stadium. Additionally, Noah Caluori, Benhard Janse van Rensburg, and George Kloska are set to make their debuts after being named on the bench for this second round fixture.

sections.heading: No fresh updates from the camp
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the England squad. Reporting from the team's preparations in the lead-up to the match against Fiji has not identified any new medical concerns or availability issues for the players selected.

sections.heading: Fiji prioritise set-piece improvement
sections.body: Interim coach Senirusi Seruvakula has named his side with Tevita Ikanivere captaining from hooker. Fiji will retain their attacking style, though the coaching staff has emphasised the need for improved discipline and set-piece stability. Levani Botia will start at No. 8, and Caleb Muntz is expected to feature at fly-half for the visitors in Liverpool.

sections.heading: Looking for first tournament points
sections.body: England enters this fixture following a loss to South Africa in the opening round. With no points currently recorded in the Nations Championship log, the team is searching for its first result of the tournament. Securing a win here is the primary focus before the squad travels to Argentina for the third round of fixtures.

sections.heading: Response, not revolution
sections.body: Staff have kept this week's message simple: composure, not panic, after the New Zealand collapse. The talk out of camp is about a calmer start and sharper game management in Australia, not a rebuild. Expect the core group backed again, with last week's disappointment used as fuel rather than an excuse heading into a demanding away test.

sections.heading: Clean bill for Saturday's Test
sections.body: No withdrawals have been added to the group since Thursday, and the touring squad remains as named for Saturday's Test. It is the kind of quiet week France will take after last weekend's physical arm-wrestle with the All Blacks — full depth, full options, and one less selection headache for the coaching staff to manage before Saturday.

sections.heading: Wallabies yet to show their hand
sections.body: Australia have not confirmed their run-on side for Saturday, so the team news column stays blank on the eve of Round 2. What is known: home conditions and an extra day's recovery after their own Round 1 outing. Nothing has been given away out of the Wallabies camp this week, and France go in assuming a full-strength selection until proven otherwise.

sections.heading: Seventh and searching for a foothold
sections.body: France sit seventh of 12 after Round 1, the unbeaten record already gone following the 32-34 loss to New Zealand, points difference sitting at -2. A first win in Australia would settle both the log and the nerves in one move; another defeat would leave the campaign needing repair from Round 3 onward. The table is early, but positions won't stay this forgiving for long.

sections.heading: Backing the same shape
sections.body: No signals of a shake-up this week — the group that got over the line against Australia travels largely intact, camp mood settled rather than triumphant. Round two brings its own exam: back it up away from home, and Ireland stop being a one-week story. That is the whole assignment heading into Japan, nothing more dressed up than that.

sections.heading: Clean sheet, for now
sections.body: Nothing fresh has landed on the injury list since Thursday's session — no new withdrawals confirmed ahead of Saturday's Test in Japan. That is the quiet good news the medical staff will take rather than shout about, given how little separated Ireland and Australia last weekend. Watch the captain's run for any late change before kickoff.

sections.heading: Japan's team sheet, still a mystery
sections.body: Japan's side for Saturday had not been confirmed as of Friday, leaving the team sheet as the week's genuine unknown. What is certain is the setting — round two on home soil, in front of a crowd with every reason to lift the hosts. Ireland will get a clearer read once teams land on Friday evening.

sections.heading: Fifth, and unfinished business
sections.body: One from one leaves Ireland fifth of 12 heading into the weekend, +2 on points difference after squeezing past Australia 33-31. Nothing separates the chasing pack this early, which is exactly why round two matters more than the table suggests — win in Japan and a promising opener becomes form, lose and it becomes a wobble.

sections.heading: Answers still under wraps
sections.body: Italy's coaches have used this week to dissect a performance that leaked 27 points to Japan in the tournament opener. No changes have been confirmed publicly, and the group that finished round one remains the base picture until a team announcement lands. The real story is where fixes get made — up front, out wide, or in game management — all under scrutiny after a tough start.

sections.heading: No fresh news to report
sections.body: No injury news has been confirmed for Saturday's Test against New Zealand, and nothing has changed on that front since yesterday. The pool that started against Japan remains the presumptive base until a team announcement lands. Any fresh knocks from that outing haven't been made public, so the group should be treated as fit and available until told otherwise.

sections.heading: New Zealand: the step up
sections.body: New Zealand arrive as a considerably bigger physical and tactical examination than Japan, with their round one form the only reliable line of reference this week. No team news for the weekend had been confirmed as this went to print, so their tournament opener remains the strongest guide available. Line speed and set-piece precision look the likely difference-makers if that pattern holds.

sections.heading: Sitting 11th of 12
sections.body: Italy go into the weekend at 11th of 12, a product of the 10-27 opening defeat to Japan and a points difference of -17. A first win of the tournament would be a genuine foothold; another loss risks compounding the pool damage before the competition reaches its business end. The table remains provisional until the round's other fixtures are played.

sections.heading: Finn Russell returns to face the world champions
sections.body: Star fly-half Finn Russell is back in the starting XV for today’s fixture at Loftus Versfeld. Coach Gregor Townsend has made adjustments to the side that defeated Argentina last weekend as Scotland look to build on their current form. Captain Sione Tuipulotu has expressed confidence that the team has evolved into the group they wanted to become, while remaining realistic about the challenge ahead.

sections.heading: No new reports from the Scotland camp
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the Scotland squad. The focus remains on the starting lineup selected for this second-round encounter, with no further updates provided on player availability since the team announcement.

sections.heading: Springboks name experienced side for Pretoria Test
sections.body: South Africa have named their lineup for today, featuring a powerful pack and a core of experienced stars including Handré Pollard, Damian de Allende, and Jesse Kriel. Stand-in captain Pieter-Steph du Toit leads the side as coach Rassie Erasmus targets another victory. The opposition comes into this meeting following a dominant 45-21 opening win over England in the Nations Championship last weekend.

sections.heading: Fifth-ranked Scots face top-ranked Springboks
sections.body: Scotland arrive at Loftus Versfeld looking to build upon their opening-round victory over Argentina, a result that moved them to fifth in the world rankings. Today’s fixture serves as a major test against the world champions, who currently sit at the top of the global standings. A win would represent a significant consolidation of Scotland's position in this competitive new tournament.

sections.heading: Steady as she goes
sections.body: Wales opened the Nations Championship with intent, banking a 39-24 win over Fiji, and the build-up to Round 2 has carried the same tone. Nothing here smells of wholesale change — a group that clicked early gets the chance to show it wasn't a one-off, with Argentina up next to test whether that form travels on the road.

sections.heading: Clean bill through Thursday
sections.body: Nothing new has emerged out of camp since Thursday, and the squad that started against Fiji remains available heading into Saturday's Test in Argentina. No knocks, no late scares, no fitness question marks — about as clean a build-up as Wales could hope for going into a Round 2 away trip.

sections.heading: Pumas bite hardest at home
sections.body: Argentina go into Round 2 on home soil, and history says the Pumas are hardest to shift there — physical up front, patient with ball in hand, happy to grind a game down. Expect their forward pack to dominate the selection talk this week, with that battle shaping as the real test of Wales's opening-round form.

sections.heading: Second of 12, for now
sections.body: One round in, Wales sit second of 12, unbeaten, with a scoring difference of plus 15 after the Fiji win. Saturday's trip to face Argentina is the first proper gauge of where this squad stands away from home — win it, and second place becomes very hard for anyone else to argue with this weekend.

sections.heading: Montoya leads side for San Juan Test
sections.body: Argentina has named its starting lineup for tonight's fixture against Wales. Julian Montoya captains the side from hooker, joined in the front row by Boris Wenger and Tomas Rapetti. Guido Petti is set to make his 100th appearance for the national team, starting in the second row alongside Matias Alemanno for the Round 2 encounter.

sections.heading: Status of Argentina squad
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the Argentina squad. Following the team announcement for this evening's match at Estadio San Juan del Bicentenario, the starting XV and replacements remain as selected by the coaching staff.

sections.heading: Wales rotate squad after Fiji win
sections.body: Wales head into this match following a 39-24 victory against Fiji. Head coach Steve Tandy has made three changes to his starting lineup, notably moving Louis Rees-Zammit to the replacements bench. Blair Murray is set to start, while hooker Ryan Elias prepares to earn his 50th international cap for the visitors.

sections.heading: Pumas seek first points of campaign
sections.body: Argentina enters this match following a 38-47 defeat to Scotland in the opening round. Currently 9 of 12 in the tournament log with a points difference of -9, the team looks to secure its first victory of the competition against Wales tonight in San Juan.

sections.heading: Fixing the finish, not the personnel
sections.body: Round 1 answered a few questions and raised plenty more. A one-point defeat to Ireland leaves the group with plenty to fix before France arrive, and inside camp the conversation this week has centred on discipline and game management in the closing quarter rather than wholesale personnel change. Expect a settled group building on last week's shape, not a reactive shake-up.

sections.heading: Camp report: unchanged since Thursday
sections.body: Nothing fresh to flag since Thursday's session — the group that trained this week remains unchanged, and no withdrawals have been added to the injury column ahead of Saturday's Test against France. Official team news will confirm who is genuinely fit versus simply available, but for now camp reports as clean as it was 24 hours ago. A quiet update is still an update.

sections.heading: France's team sheet still to land
sections.body: France's team news for Saturday hadn't landed by the time of writing, so the exact French XV is this morning's open question — check the app once it drops. This is a side with a reputation for setting terms up front, and Saturday's sheet will show how much of that stays intact through a rotated bench. Worth another look before kickoff.

sections.heading: Friday's log: eighth of 12, all to play for
sections.body: As Friday's table stands, Australia sit eighth of 12 after the Round 1 stumble against Ireland — W0 D0 L1, 31 points for, 33 against, a net differential of -2. Beat France and that early ledger starts looking like a blip; lose again and the gap to the top of the log stretches further. Saturday carries more weight than a routine Round 2 fixture.

sections.heading: Jones returns as Japan names lineup
sections.body: Eddie Jones returns as head coach following a four-match suspension. For this fixture, Japan has made one change to the starting side that defeated Italy, with winger Taira Main replacing Kippei Ishida. Ryunosuke Ito continues in the fly-half position for the team as they look to build on their opening tournament victory.

sections.heading: Injury report
sections.body: No fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the Japan squad. The opposition has dealt with the loss of Robert Baloucoune, who was a late withdrawal from their previous match due to a hamstring injury.

sections.heading: Ireland reveals nine changes
sections.body: Ireland head coach Andy Farrell has made nine changes to his starting side following their victory against Australia. Tadhg Beirne captains the team for the first time, while Ciarán Frawley is set to start at out-half. The visitors have included four uncapped players in their matchday 23 as they look to build on their opening tournament win.

sections.heading: Round 2 implications
sections.body: Japan enters this contest following a 27-10 win over Italy in the opening round. A victory today would see Japan move to two wins from two matches in the Nations Championship. Following this fixture, Japan is scheduled to host France in Tokyo, while Ireland will travel to Auckland to face New Zealand.

sections.heading: All Blacks name side with new cap included
sections.body: The All Blacks have named their starting side to face Italy in Wellington. Ardie Savea will start at No. 8, with Codie Taylor captaining the team from hooker. Anton Segner is included among the reserves and is in line to become the first Germany-born player to earn a cap for the All Blacks.

sections.heading: No fresh updates from the camp
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the All Blacks squad. Scott Barrett remains unavailable for the team due to a back injury that was previously noted when the wider squad was first selected for this series.

sections.heading: Italy makes three changes to their starting lineup
sections.body: Italy has confirmed three changes to their starting side following their opening defeat to Japan. Head coach Gonzalo Quesada has recalled fullback Tommaso Allan and wing Louis Lynagh into the starting XV. The side is led by captain Michele Lamaro, who starts at flanker for the Azzurri as they seek their first win of the tournament.

sections.heading: All Blacks seek points in second round
sections.body: New Zealand enters this round holding four competition points after their opening win against France. Sitting second in the Southern Hemisphere table, the team is looking to build on their performance in the inaugural Nations Championship. A win in this match is essential for New Zealand to maintain their position within the conference standings before heading to the northern hemisphere.

sections.heading: Springboks name starting side for second round
sections.body: South Africa have named their starting XV to face Scotland at Loftus Versfeld. The lineup features Boan Venter, Johan Grobbelaar, and Wilco Louw in the front row, with Cobus Wiese and Ruan Nortje in the second row. Paul de Villiers, Pieter-Steph du Toit, and Evan Roos complete the pack, while Embrose Papier and Handré Pollard partner in the halves.

sections.heading: No fresh injury reports in team coverage
sections.body: There is no new information regarding player fitness or fresh injury concerns in the reports covering the South African squad today. The team selections as announced on Thursday remain the current reference point for the personnel available for this fixture. The coaching staff has not issued updates regarding any changes to the match-day group since the initial squad announcement.

sections.heading: Scotland includes returning fly-half for Pretoria test
sections.body: Scotland have confirmed their starting lineup, headlined by the return of fly-half Finn Russell following his recovery from a calf injury. Captain Sione Tuipulotu leads the side, which arrives in Pretoria after a win against Argentina. Head coach Gregor Townsend describes the match as a significant test, noting the difficulty of competing against the Springboks at their home ground.

sections.heading: Springboks look to maintain momentum in round 2
sections.body: Following a 45-21 victory over England in the opening round, the team currently leads the tournament log with 1 win and a positive points difference of 24. A successful result against Scotland today would consolidate this position at the top of the table as the competition progresses through the second round of the Nations Championship.
